title: Spotted Dick

overview.description: A traditional steamed British suet pudding packed with currants and gently flavoured with lemon and spices.

method: Place the flour, suet, currants, sugar, lemon zest, cinnamon, nutmeg and salt in a large bowl.
Add the milk and vanilla extract.
Stir until a soft, sticky dough forms.
Spoon the mixture into the pudding basin.
Cover the basin securely with baking parchment and foil.
Place the basin in a large saucepan.
Pour in boiling water until it reaches approximately halfway up the sides of the basin.
Cover the saucepan with a lid.
Steam for approximately 2 hours, topping up the water with boiling water if necessary.
Turn the pudding out onto a serving plate.

learning.did-you-know: The name 'spotted dick' refers to the spots made by the dried fruit, while 'dick' is an old dialect form of pudding.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How suet creates a light steamed pudding and how moist heat transforms a simple dough into a tender dessert.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A classic old-fashioned British pudding that is inexpensive, filling, freezer-friendly and ideal for the family's weekly pudding tradition.
chapman-tips: Do not overmix the dough once the liquid is added.
The dough should be soft and sticky rather than dry.
Keep the water at a gentle simmer rather than a violent boil.
Make sure the pudding basin is well covered so water does not enter.
Check the water level during steaming and top up with boiling water if necessary.
Serve immediately for the best texture.
A little extra lemon zest gives the pudding a brighter flavour.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Cool completely, wrap securely and freeze in portions.
notes.leftovers: Reheat by steaming or gently in the microwave and serve with custard.
